name: security-gym-v4
description: Ground-truth-labeled Linux server log and eBPF kernel-event streams for cybersecurity continual reinforcement learning research. The v4 dataset contains 11.2M base benign events (real Linux server traffic from four personal servers plus 24-hour eBPF kernel-event collections from three Debian 13 servers) plus 60,468 attack campaign events across six MITRE ATT&CK-aligned attack modules executed against a purpose-built lab VM. Composed into four pre-mixed experiment streams ranging from 4.9M to 257.7M events with Poisson-scheduled attack injection. Designed for use with the security-gym Gymnasium environment (`SecurityLogStream-Text-v0` and `SecurityLogStream-Hybrid-v0` observation modes).
citeAs: @dataset{lawson_security_gym_dataset_2026, author = {Lawson, Keith and Malik, Hafiz}, title = {Security-Gym Dataset: Labeled Linux Log and eBPF Streams for Continual Learning Research}, version = {4.0}, year = {2026}, doi = {10.5281/zenodo.19482383}, url = {https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19482383}, license = {Apache-2.0}}
rai:dataCollection: Benign traffic was collected from four personal Linux servers running standard services (SSH, nginx, syslog) operated by the dataset author. eBPF kernel events were collected over 24-hour windows on three Debian 13 servers (a hypervisor, a public web server, and a GPU lab server) via BCC tracepoints (sys_enter_execve, sched_process_exit, sys_enter_connect, sys_enter_accept4, sys_enter_openat, sys_enter_unlinkat). Attack traffic was generated by 10 scripted campaigns executed by a purpose-built campaign orchestrator against a deliberately vulnerable Debian 11 lab VM. No third-party infrastructure was probed.
rai:dataCollectionRawData: Raw text log lines (auth.log, syslog, nginx access/error) and raw eBPF event dumps. Stored verbatim in the events.raw_line column.
rai:syntheticDataGeneration: All malicious events are synthetic, generated by a custom Python campaign orchestrator that executes 10 scripted MITRE ATT&CK-aligned attack campaigns (six modules: brute_force, web_attack, recon, malware, dos, lateral_movement) against an isolated UTM-virtualised Debian 11 lab VM. Benign events are real Linux server logs (auth.log, syslog, nginx access/error) and eBPF kernel events recorded from the dataset author's own servers, then PII-scrubbed and screened with MaliciousFilter to remove signature-matched attacks. Composed experiment streams further inject the synthetic attack events into the real benign baseline at MITRE ATT&CK-weighted Poisson-scheduled rates via StreamComposer. No third-party traffic, no production telemetry, no LLM-generated content.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Attack labeling uses CampaignLabeler with time-window + source-IP matching: an event is labeled malicious if its timestamp falls within an attack phase's window AND its src_ip matches an attacker IP for that phase. Events with no src_ip (e.g., syslog daemon messages) match on time window alone. eBPF kernel events use the same labeler. Benign events are filtered with MaliciousFilter using deterministic web/auth signature rules (path traversal, SQL injection, XSS, JNDI injection, RFI, scanner user-agents, failed-password sequences) before inclusion in the benign baseline. PII scrubbing applies case-insensitive hostname/domain/IP replacement to a single synthetic target identity (`isildur` / `192.168.2.201`).
rai:dataAnnotationPlatform: Custom Python pipeline (campaign orchestrator + CampaignLabeler + MaliciousFilter)
rai:dataAnnotationAnalysis: All composed streams pass nine automated validation checks: (1) label consistency (is_malicious=1 implies non-NULL attack_type/stage/severity), (2) raw-line spot checks via regex per attack type, (3) campaign boundary containment, (4) campaign-type cross-validation, (5) target-array consistency for the SecurityGymStream adapter, (6) attack-type distribution against composition weights, (7) temporal order monotonicity, (8) no unlabeled events, (9) session coherence (all events in a session share the same is_malicious label).
rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: Compressed with Zstandard. Composed experiment streams are produced by StreamComposer with per-stream MITRE ATT&CK-aligned Poisson-scheduled attack injection and 24.2% eBPF downsampling.
rai:dataReleaseMaintenancePlan: Versioned via Zenodo (concept DOI 10.5281/zenodo.18901541 always resolves to latest; v4 frozen at 10.5281/zenodo.19482383). HuggingFace mirror updated on each Zenodo version bump. Issues tracked at https://github.com/j-klawson/security-gym/issues.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: None. PII scrubbing maps all real-server hostnames, domains, and IP addresses to a single synthetic target identity (`isildur` / `192.168.2.201`). Network events from detected malicious IPs are excluded from the benign baseline before release. Source IPs in the published data are either the synthetic target identity or attacker-aliased subnets generated by the campaign orchestrator.
rai:dataBiases: Single-host scope (one target server). Benign baselines reflect the dataset author's personal infrastructure (academic / Linux developer profile), not enterprise fleet behavior. Benign load patterns may underrepresent enterprise SOC telemetry diversity.
rai:dataLimitations: Six attack modules cover only a small subset of the MITRE ATT&CK technique space. Persistence, privilege escalation, and exfiltration modules are planned but not yet implemented. The benchmark does not test zero-day generalization (all attack modules are known TTPs) or multi-host lateral movement. The defense state (blocklist, throttle list, isolation mode) is simulated within the environment, not connected to a real firewall. eBPF events are downsampled to 24.2% during stream composition to simulate a single busy server, preserving event-type distributions but losing some inter-event temporal correlation.
rai:dataUseCases: Streaming continual reinforcement learning research; online representation learning from raw text and structured kernel-event streams; streaming deep-RL technique validation (LMS, ObGD, EMA normalization, sparse initialization); reward design in observation-suppressing environments; comparative evaluation of detection approaches (learned vs. rule-based) on non-stationary data.
rai:dataNotIntendedUseCases: Production SOC tooling. Models of enterprise infrastructure. Zero-day generalization evaluation. Substitutes for real-world deployment validation. Multi-host lateral-movement studies. The bundled attack modules target a purpose-built lab VM (`isildur`) and must not be directed at third-party infrastructure.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Research benchmark for continual learning on real cybersecurity telemetry. Releases otherwise unavailable PII-scrubbed Linux server traces with both traditional log channels and eBPF kernel events to enable academic reproduction of streaming-RL results in cybersecurity defense.

prov:wasDerivedFrom.name: mitre-attack-enterprise-matrix
prov:wasDerivedFrom.description: Source taxonomy of adversary tactics and techniques. Each Security-Gym attack campaign maps to one or more ATT&CK technique IDs (T1110 brute force, T1190 web exploitation, T1046 service discovery, T1059.004 unix shell, T1499 endpoint DoS, T1021 remote services). Used as the conceptual seed for the synthetic attack campaign design and for the per-stream attack-type distributions in StreamComposer. License compatibility: MITRE ATT&CK terms permit redistribution and derivative works with attribution; Security-Gym only inherits ATT&CK technique identifiers, not the underlying ATT&CK content.

prov:wasDerivedFrom.name: security-gym-campaigns
prov:wasDerivedFrom.alternateName: Security-Gym campaign configurations and orchestrator (synthetic-data seeds)
prov:wasDerivedFrom.description: Per-campaign YAML configurations specifying attack phases, timing profiles (constant, accelerating, burst), and target identities, plus the Python campaign orchestrator that consumes them. These two artifacts are the deterministic seeds for the synthetic attack events in this dataset: reproducible regeneration of any released stream requires only the configs, the orchestrator code, and a fixed RNG seed. No language models or other generative models were used in attack-event synthesis.

prov:wasDerivedFrom.name: security-gym-first-party-linux-logs
prov:wasDerivedFrom.alternateName: First-party Linux server logs from author-operated infrastructure
prov:wasDerivedFrom.description: Benign baseline events were collected from four Linux servers operated by the dataset author over multi-month windows; no upstream public dataset was used. Server identities are scrubbed and replaced with the synthetic target identity (isildur / 192.168.2.201) before release. The first-party origin means no external license restrictions are inherited; the released benign data is offered under the same Apache-2.0 terms as the rest of the dataset.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: Benign telemetry collection
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Benign Linux server logs (auth.log, syslog, nginx access/error) collected over multi-month windows from four author-operated servers. eBPF kernel events (sys_enter_execve, sched_process_exit, sys_enter_connect, sys_enter_accept4, sys_enter_openat, sys_enter_unlinkat) collected over 24-hour windows on three Debian 13 servers (a hypervisor, a public web server, and a GPU lab server) via BCC tracepoints. No third-party infrastructure was probed; no crowdworkers or external annotators involved.
prov:wasGeneratedBy.platform: Native Linux logging facilities (rsyslog, OpenSSH, nginx) plus a BCC-based eBPF agent.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: Synthetic attack-event generation
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Adversary events generated by a custom Python campaign orchestrator (security_gym/campaigns/) executing 10 scripted MITRE ATT&CK-aligned campaigns against an isolated UTM-virtualised Debian 11 lab VM. Six attack modules are released (brute_force, web_attack, recon, malware, dos, lateral_movement). All attack traffic is the deterministic output of the YAML-configured orchestrator under a fixed RNG seed. No language models, no generative AI, no human attackers; the orchestrator is purely procedural Python with explicit phase scripts.

prov:wasAssociatedWith.name: Security-Gym campaign orchestrator
prov:wasAssociatedWith.agentType: Procedural Python (no LLM, no generative model)
prov:wasAssociatedWith.validation: Every released stream passes nine automated validation checks (see Validation activity below), including raw-line regex spot checks per attack type and attack-type distribution checks against composition weights.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.platform: Custom Python campaign orchestrator targeting a UTM-virtualised Debian 11 lab VM (isildur, 192.168.2.201).

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: Automated labelling and benign-baseline filtering
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Attack labelling via CampaignLabeler with time-window plus source-IP matching against orchestrator-recorded attack phases (events with no src_ip match on time window alone). Benign-baseline pre-screening via MaliciousFilter using deterministic web/auth signature rules: path traversal, SQL injection, XSS, JNDI injection, RFI, scanner user-agents, failed-password sequences. No human annotators, no crowdworkers; all annotation is fully automated and deterministic given the inputs.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: PII scrubbing, compression, and stream composition
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: PII scrubbing applies case-insensitive hostname/domain/IP replacement to a single synthetic target identity (isildur / 192.168.2.201). Source IPs from detected attackers in the benign baseline are excluded before release. Storage is Zstandard-compressed SQLite. Composed experiment streams are produced by StreamComposer with per-stream MITRE ATT&CK-aligned Poisson-scheduled attack injection and 24.2% eBPF downsampling.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: Automated validation of released streams
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Each composed stream passes nine automated validation checks: (1) label consistency (is_malicious=1 implies non-NULL attack_type/stage/severity), (2) raw-line spot checks via regex per attack type, (3) campaign boundary containment, (4) campaign-type cross-validation, (5) target-array consistency for the SecurityGymStream adapter, (6) attack-type distribution against composition weights, (7) temporal order monotonicity, (8) no unlabelled events, (9) session coherence (all events in a session share the same is_malicious label).

distribution.name: exp_7d_brute_v4.db.zst
distribution.description: 7-day SSH brute-force smoke-test stream: 4,891,541 events (25,980 malicious). Decompressed: 1.9 GB SQLite database.

distribution.name: exp_30d_heavy_v4.db.zst
distribution.description: 30-day default benchmark stream: 21,511,208 events (609,520 malicious) with uniform 0.20 mix of discovery, brute_force, web_exploit, credential_stuffing, execution at 10.0 campaigns/day. Decompressed: 8.5 GB SQLite database.

distribution.name: exp01_90d_v4.db.zst
distribution.description: 90-day eBPF feature ablation stream: 63,212,997 events (549,787 malicious) with discovery 0.35, brute_force 0.30, web_exploit 0.20, credential_stuffing 0.10, execution 0.05 distribution at 3.0 campaigns/day. Decompressed: 25 GB SQLite database. Used in the §6.3 30-seed eBPF ablation (Cohen's d=2.577).

distribution.name: exp_365d_realistic_v4.db.zst
distribution.description: 365-day long-horizon stability stream: 257,654,256 events (1,857,178 malicious) with discovery 0.30, brute_force 0.30, credential_stuffing 0.20, web_exploit 0.15, execution 0.05 distribution at 2.5 campaigns/day. Decompressed: 101 GB SQLite database. Hosted on Zenodo (not on HuggingFace) due to size.

distribution.name: benign_v4.db.zst
distribution.description: Base benign-traffic database: 11,159,241 events (7.9M server logs from four servers plus 3.24M eBPF events from 24-hour collections on three Debian 13 servers). PII-scrubbed. Hosted on Zenodo (not on HuggingFace).

distribution.name: campaigns_v2.db.zst
distribution.description: Base attack-campaign database: 60,468 events (30,436 malicious, 30,032 benign) across 10 campaigns spanning 9 single-module + multi-stage configurations. Hosted on Zenodo (not on HuggingFace).

recordSet.name: events
recordSet.description: All streams share the same `events` table schema (single-table SQLite database). Each row is one telemetry event with ground-truth labels.

field.name: id
field.description: Auto-incrementing event identifier within the database.

field.name: timestamp
field.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of the event.

field.name: raw_line
field.description: Original log line or eBPF event text as captured.

field.name: parsed
field.description: Optional JSON-encoded parsed fields (NULL when not parsed).

field.name: attack_type
field.description: One of `brute_force` (T1110.001), `credential_stuffing` (T1110.004), `discovery` (T1046), `web_exploit` (T1190), or `execution` (T1059.004). NULL for benign events.

field.name: attack_stage
field.description: MITRE ATT&CK kill-chain stage label. NULL for benign events.

field.name: severity
field.description: Threat severity 1-5. NULL for benign events.

field.name: session_id
field.description: Session identifier for events that map to a session (e.g., SSH sessions).

field.name: src_ip
field.description: Source IP address (PII-scrubbed for benign events).

field.name: username
field.description: Username on authentication and session events.
